request.question: Which genus of plant had more species identified with it in the past, Carissa or Aruncus?

title: Carissa
Aruncus
Synodontis omias
Aruncus dioicus
Tephritid Workers Database
IUCN Red List extinct in the wild species
Ornithogalum lebaense
Verrucomicrobia
Lancefield grouping
Platanthera azorica
sentences: Carissa is a genus of shrubs or small trees native to tropical and subtropical regions of Africa, Australia and Asia.
Until recently about 100 species were listed, but most of them have been relegated to the status of synonyms or assigned to other genera, such as "Acokanthera".
Aruncus is a genus of clump forming herbaceous perennial plants in the family Rosaceae.
Botanical opinion of the number of species differs, with from one to four species accepted.
They are closely related to the genera "Filipendula" and "Spiraea", and are native to mountainous damp woodland in temperate regions of the Northern Hemisphere.
Creamy white plumes of flowers are produced above veined and toothed leaflets.
Synodontis omias is a species of upside-down catfish native to the Niger River basin of Guinea, Mali, Niger and Nigeria.
It was first described by German-born British zoologist, ichthyologist, and herpetologist Albert Günther in 1864, from a specimen collected in Jebba, Nigeria, on the Niger River.
There have been very few specimens of this species identified, and it may be the same species as "S. budgetti" from the Upper Niger.
Aruncus dioicus (Walt.)
(known as goat's beard, buck's-beard or bride's feathers) is a flowering herbaceous perennial plant in the family Rosaceae, and is the type species of the genus "Aruncus".
It has alternate, pinnately compound leaves, on thin, stiff stems, with plumes of feathery white or cream flowers borne in summer.
This plant can be found in moist woodland, often at higher altitudes, throughout temperate areas of Europe, Asia, and eastern and western North America.
In the UK it is considered suitable for planting in and around water areas, and has gained the Royal Horticultural Society's Award of Garden Merit.
The Tephritid Workers Database is a web-based database for sharing information on tephritid fruit flies.

Because these species are one of the most economically important group of insect species that threaten fruit and vegetable production and trade worldwide, a tremendous amount of information is made available each year: new technologies developed, new information on their biology and ecology; new control methods made available, new species identified, new outbreaks recorded and new operational control programmes launched.
The TWD allows workers to keep up-to-date on the most recent developments and provides an easily accessible and always available resource.
Version 2014.2 of the IUCN Red List of Threatened Species identified 78 (39 animal, 39 plant) extinct in the wild species, subspecies and varieties, stocks and subpopulations.
Ornithogalum lebaense is a flowering plant that grows in southwest Angola.
It was discovered on January 2009 by Ernst Jacobus van Jaarsveld.
The word ""lebaense"" refers to Leba Pass, where the plant lives.
"O. lebaense" does not have a known common name, most likely because its habitat is not one that is easily accessible by local people.
There are three other "Ornithogalum" species identified in Angola: "Ornithogalum tenuifolium", "O. pulchrum" and "O. benguellens".
Verrucomicrobia is a phylum of bacteria.
This phylum contains only a few described species ("Verrucomicrobium spinosum", is an example, the phylum is named after this).
The species identified have been isolated from fresh water and soil environments and human faeces.

A number of as-yet uncultivated species have been identified in association with eukaryotic hosts including extrusive explosive ectosymbionts of protists and endosymbionts of nematodes residing in their gametes.
While verrucae is another name for the warts often found on hands and feet, this phylum is so called not because it is a causative agent thereof, but because of its wart-like morphology.
Lancefield grouping is a method of grouping catalase-negative, coagulase-negative bacteria based on the carbohydrate composition of bacterial antigens found on their cell walls.

The system, created by Rebecca Lancefield, was historically used to organize the various members of the family Streptococcaceae, which includes the genera Lactococcus and Streptococcus, but now is largely superfluous due to explosive growth in the number of streptococcal species identified since the 1970s.
As of 2016, Lancefield designations are often used to communicate medical microbiological results in the United States.

Enterococcus, formerly known as Group D Streptococcus, was believed to be a member of the genus Streptococcus until 1984, after the Lancefield criteria were devised, and so were included in the original Lancefield grouping.
Many - but not all - species of streptococcus are beta-hemolytic.
Notably, Enterococcus and Streptococcus bovis (Lancefield Group D) are not beta-hemolytic.

Though there are many groups of streptococcus, only five are known to commonly cause disease in immune-competent human beings: Group A, Group B, both members of Group D, and two groups that lack the Lancefield carbohydrate antigen: Streptococcus pneumoniae and Viridans streptococci.
Platanthera azorica, commonly known as Hochstetter's butterfly orchid, is a species of orchid in the genus "Platanthera".
It was "rediscovered" in 2013 on a single volcanic ridge on the central Azores island of São Jorge, and "is arguably Europe's rarest "bona fide" orchid species."

While "P. azorica" was previously considered by some sources to be the same as "Platanthera micrantha", the 2013 discovery triggered a thorough analysis of historic and current data and specimens, resulting in three species identified.

candidate.summarize1.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `passages`, produce the fields `summary`.

Role and goal: Summarize the first-hop evidence so the next agent can find the missing second-hop information. Produce a single short, factual `summary` that: (1) compresses the grounded facts in the provided passages; (2) exposes the bridge entity, the specific missing fact, or the retrieval direction needed for the next hop; and (3) gives concrete evidence anchors (quoted or clearly paraphrased phrases) that a retriever can use.

Content rules:
- Keep the summary concise and fact-focused. Do not attempt to produce the final answer; describe only what the passages support and what is still needed.
- Preserve the same passage facts and phrasing as you would otherwise (you may quote or paraphrase). Do not change which facts are exposed; only condense them.
- You may combine multiple explicit, identity-aligned facts from the passages to state a bridge or to justify a next-hop direction. Do not invent facts, infer new temporal facts, or treat composition as unsupported when the necessary premises are present.
- If the passages lack a required premise, or contain conflicting identity links, explicitly state which premise or identity link is missing or ambiguous so the next agent can retrieve or disambiguate it.

Date-format rule (narrow formatting change):
- When the summary includes a date token that appears in the passages (any calendar date or parenthetical date taken from the text), render that date using the canonical form FullMonthName day, year (for example, "September 27, 2017") inside the evidence description. This normalization only changes the string form of the date token; it must not add, infer, or imply any additional temporal facts, nor be used as a reason to produce a final answer.
- Otherwise, leave all other wording and evidence presentation unchanged. Preserve quotations or clear paraphrases of the same passage text; only adjust the date token's string format.

Output contract:
- Return exactly one field `summary` containing the compressed first-hop evidence, the bridge/missing-fact signal, and any retrieval direction needed for the next hop. Do not output final answers, extra metadata, or new fields.
candidate.create_query_hop2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, produce the fields `query`.

Produce exactly one compact retrieval query string (single line) in the `query` field and nothing else. The query should be a concise keyword-style search targeted to recover the missing bridge or supporting evidence needed by downstream agents.

General behavior:
- Use the visible facts in summary_1 as anchors (album titles, artist names, dates, relation terms) and compose a focused query that is likely to retrieve passages supporting the needed relation. Prefer concrete anchors and relation keywords over full sentences.
- Do not include explanatory text, user-facing prompts, or requests-for-clarification in the `query` field. Do not invent or assert missing facts (for example, do not supply an artist name that is not present in summary_1).
- Preserve the single-query output contract: emit exactly one string and no other fields or commentary.

Special-case for missing album artist or genre (narrow semantic boundary):
- If summary_1 shows an album title but does not include the album's artist or genre, or if summary_1 explicitly signals inability to determine genre or explicitly asks for the artist/passage (phrases such as "can't determine" or "please provide the artist"), then emit exactly one compact search phrase formatted as:
  <AlbumTitle> album artist genre
  Use only the visible album title as it appears in summary_1 followed by the three keywords: album artist genre. Do not add punctuation, explanatory words, alternate tokens, or candidate artist names.
- Do not apply this special-case if summary_1 already contains the artist or genre, or if the album anchor is an ambiguous short token that requires human clarification.

Fallback and downstream behavior:
- If retrieval using the emitted query returns no useful passages, the downstream agent may then ask the user for clarification or additional context. Do not ask the user inside the `query` field.
candidate.summarize2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `context`, `passages`, produce the field `summary`.

Task: Integrate the first-hop `context` with the newly retrieved `passages` and produce a single concise `summary` that preserves the facts needed by the final answer. Resolve the earlier bridge when the supplied passages support doing so; retain uncertainty only when the post-retrieval evidence is genuinely insufficient, ambiguous, or conflicting.

Guidelines:
- Treat a concise answer in `context` as the primary claim. Do not replace that concise context answer unless the `passages` contain a direct, identity-aligned contradiction.
- Before attributing any fact from a passage to the entity or relation named in `context`, confirm identity alignment (clear aliasing or explicit linking). Do not assume two similarly named mentions are the same without such alignment.
- If the `passages` consistently corroborate the context claim and are identity-aligned, append one brief paraphrased supporting sentence that cites the corroboration (keep the summary compact).
- If the `passages` are weak, ambiguous, or conflict with each other or with the context claim (but do not provide an explicit, identity-aligned contradiction), preserve the context claim and append a single short clause that flags the uncertainty or contradiction. When flagging, you may optionally include a verbatim quoted phrase from a passage of up to 10 words to show the source wording. Do not invent resolutions beyond what the `passages` show.
- If the `passages` contain an explicit, identity-aligned contradiction to the context claim, state that the passages contradict the context claim and briefly note the conflicting passage fact (optionally with a ≤10-word quote). Only in this case may the summary stop asserting the context claim as definitive.
- Keep the `summary` compact (aim for 1–3 sentences). Use uncertainty language only when a required premise, identity link, or relation is genuinely missing, ambiguous, or conflicting. Do not default to uncertainty when composition across context and passages licenses a conclusion.
- You may combine multiple explicit, identity-aligned facts from `context` and `passages` to form a licensed multi-hop conclusion; the supporting facts need not all appear in a single passage.
- Do not include long verbatim quotes, do not invent facts, and do not attempt to resolve contradictions beyond reporting them as present in the `passages`.

Output: a single `summary` string that follows the rules above.
candidate.final_answer.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, `summary_2`, produce the field `answer`.

Use only the information in the provided summaries as evidence. summary_2 is the later, integrated evidence state and should be the primary source for deciding the minimal answer; summary_1 may contain useful first-hop compression or retrieval-oriented language and may be used to supply premises that, together with summary_2, license a conclusion. Do not invent facts or use outside knowledge.

Emit only the minimal answer string supported by the visible summaries in the `answer` field. Do not include explanations, justification, or any additional text.

Preserve multi-hop composition: you may combine multiple explicit, identity-aligned facts across the summaries to form a licensed conclusion even when no single sentence states the whole relation. Distinguish licensed composition from unsupported inference; emit uncertainty only when a required premise, identity link, or relation is genuinely missing, ambiguous, or conflicting in the summaries.

Minimal-answer formatting rule (explicit):
- If the summaries jointly support one minimal literal and that literal appears verbatim in the summaries and consists of multiple contiguous tokens (for example: a full personal name, a full date, or a place name), emit that full literal exactly as it appears in the summaries (verbatim, unchanged). Do not truncate or coerce it to a single token.
- If the jointly supported minimal answer is itself a single-token label, emit exactly that single token (in lowercase) and nothing else.

When in doubt about whether the summaries license a single definitive literal, prefer to emit uncertainty only if the required supporting premise or identity link is actually missing, ambiguous, or conflicting. Do not make uncertainty the default when composition or transitive reasoning is required and the necessary premises are present.

Ensure the `answer` field contains only the minimal answer string supported by the summaries (subject to the formatting rule above).

response.hop1_query: Which genus of plant had more species identified with it in the past, Carissa or Aruncus?
hop1_docs: Aruncus dioicus | Aruncus dioicus (Walt.)  (known as goat's beard, buck's-beard or bride's feathers) is a flowering herbaceous perennial plant in the family Rosaceae, and is the type species of the genus "Aruncus".  It has alternate, pinnately compound leaves, on thin, stiff stems, with plumes of feathery white or cream flowers borne in summer.  This plant can be found in moist woodland, often at higher altitudes, throughout temperate areas of Europe, Asia, and eastern and western North America.  In the UK it is considered suitable for planting in and around water areas, and has gained the Royal Horticultural Society's Award of Garden Merit.

Aruncus | Aruncus is a genus of clump forming herbaceous perennial plants in the family Rosaceae.  Botanical opinion of the number of species differs, with from one to four species accepted.  They are closely related to the genera "Filipendula" and "Spiraea", and are native to mountainous damp woodland in temperate regions of the Northern Hemisphere.  Creamy white plumes of flowers are produced above veined and toothed leaflets.

Binomial nomenclature | Binomial nomenclature (also called binominal nomenclature or binary nomenclature) is a formal system of naming species of living things by giving each a name composed of two parts, both of which use Latin grammatical forms, although they can be based on words from other languages.  Such a name is called a binomial name (which may be shortened to just "binomial"), a binomen, binominal name or a scientific name; more informally it is also called a Latin name.  The first part of the name identifies the genus to which the species belongs; the second part identifies the species within the genus.  For example, humans belong to the genus "Homo" and within this genus to the species "Homo sapiens".  The "formal" introduction of this system of naming species is credited to Carl Linnaeus, effectively beginning with his work "Species Plantarum" in 1753.  But Gaspard Bauhin, in as early as 1623, had introduced in his book "Pinax theatri botanici" (English, "Illustrated exposition of plants") many names of genera that were later adopted by Linnaeus.

Cyrtandra (plant) | Cyrtandra is a genus of flowering plants containing about 600 species, with more being discovered often, and is thus the largest genus in the family Gesneriaceae.  These plants are native to Southeast Asia, Australia, and the Pacific Islands, with the centre of diversity in Southeast Asia and the Malesian region.  The genus is common, but many species within it are very rare, localized, endangered endemic plants.  The species can be difficult to identify because they are highly polymorphic and because they readily hybridize with each other.  The plants may be small herbs, vines, shrubs, epiphytes, or trees.  The genus is characterized in part by having two stamens, and most species have white flowers, with a few red-, orange-, yellow- and pink-flowered species known.  Almost all species live in rainforest habitats.

Securinega | Securinega is a genus of plants in the family Phyllanthaceae, first described as a genus in 1789.  As presently conceived, the genus is native to Madagascar and the Mascarene Islands in the Indian Ocean.  In the past, it was considered to be much more widespread, thus explaining the long list of species formerly included.

Carissa | Carissa is a genus of shrubs or small trees native to tropical and subtropical regions of Africa, Australia and Asia.  Until recently about 100 species were listed, but most of them have been relegated to the status of synonyms or assigned to other genera, such as "Acokanthera".

Machlomovirus | Machlomovirus is a genus of plant viruses, in the family Tombusviridae.  Plants serve as natural hosts.  There is currently only one species in this genus: the type species "Maize chlorotic mottle virus" (MCMV), which causes significant losses in maize production worldwide.  MCMV was first identified in the U.S. state of Kansas causing corn lethal necrosis, a severe disease that negatively affects all stages of development for maize plants.
response.summary_1: Evidence: Carissa — "Until recently about 100 species were listed, but most of them have been relegated to the status of synonyms or assigned to other genera, such as 'Acokanthera'." (passage [6]). Aruncus — "Botanical opinion of the number of species differs, with from one to four species accepted." (passage [2]). Bridge/missing fact: confirm the historical timeframe and sources for Carissa's "about 100 species" (what period "until recently" refers to) and the taxonomic revisions that reduced that count (e.g., lists or papers showing species transferred to Acokanthera). Retrieval direction: retrieve taxonomic revisions or historical species lists for Carissa and Aruncus (especially records documenting the ~100 listed Carissa species and the dates/authors of reassignments) to confirm which genus had more species historically.
response.hop2_query: Carissa "about 100 species" taxonomic revision Acokanthera historical species list Aruncus "one to four species"
hop2_docs: Carissa | Carissa is a genus of shrubs or small trees native to tropical and subtropical regions of Africa, Australia and Asia.  Until recently about 100 species were listed, but most of them have been relegated to the status of synonyms or assigned to other genera, such as "Acokanthera".

Aruncus | Aruncus is a genus of clump forming herbaceous perennial plants in the family Rosaceae.  Botanical opinion of the number of species differs, with from one to four species accepted.  They are closely related to the genera "Filipendula" and "Spiraea", and are native to mountainous damp woodland in temperate regions of the Northern Hemisphere.  Creamy white plumes of flowers are produced above veined and toothed leaflets.

List of birds of Karnataka | The Indian state of Karnataka has about 531 bird species within its boundaries.  The following list of birds is based on multiple sources and incorporates recent taxonomic revisions.  Species which are considered provisional, introduced or escapees are mentioned, but are not considered in the count.

Jellynose fish | The jellynose fishes or tadpole fishes are the small order Ateleopodiformes.  This group of ray-finned fish is monotypic, containing a single family Ateleopodidae.  It has about a dozen species in four genera, but these enigmatic fishes are in need of taxonomic revision.

List of birds of Andhra Pradesh | The Indian state of Andhra Pradesh has about 484 bird species within its political boundary.  This following list of birds is based on the taxonomic treatment of Clements's 5th edition but includes recent revisions.  The Indian roller is the state bird of Andhra Pradesh.

Calumma nasutum | Calumma nasutum, the nose-horned chameleon, is a small species of chameleon found in Madagascar.  The taxonomic identity of the species is currently uncertain and in need of revision, and this revision is likely to result in several newly described species.

Hygrophila (plant) | Hygrophila, commonly known as swampweeds, is a genus of flowering plants in the acanthus family, Acanthaceae.  There are about 80 to 100 species, of which many are aquatic plants.  The genus is distributed across the tropical and subtropical world.  It is one of only two genera in its family that contains aquatic plants, the other being "Justicia".  The genus is treated in the tribe "Hygrophileae", which is noted as being in need of revision at the genus level, meaning the current taxonomic boundaries of "Hygrophila" are likely to change in the future.
hop2_titles: List of birds of Andhra Pradesh
response.summary_2: Carissa historically had many more species associated with it—“about 100 species were listed” until recent revisions (many now synonyms or reassigned to genera like Acokanthera)—whereas Aruncus has only one to four accepted species; therefore Carissa had more species identified with it in the past.
